coredata.dc:title: Photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive room temperature phosphorescence carbon dot-based composites with excellent reversibility and water resistance

coredata.dc:description: Photo-stimulation responsive room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have potential applications in advanced dynamic information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. However, the development of photo-responsive long-wavelength RTP CD materials with excellent reversibility and water resistance, as well as CD materials with both photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP properties, remains a challenge. Here, pyrene, which has a large conjugated structure, and 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde, which has a tetraphenylethylene structure, were selected as the main raw materials to synthesize two CDs. Then, by introducing a PMMA matrix, reversible photo-responsive long-wavelength red RTP emission with a lifetime of 257 ms from a CD-based composite is realized for the first time, and the combination of photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible yellow RTP with a lifetime of 287 ms is realized. Importantly, photo-responsive reversible RTP has excellent water resistance. The fluorescence discoloration was caused by photocyclization under long-term UV irradiation due to the presence of the tetraphenylethylene structure. The rigid environment provided by the PMMA matrix and the hydrogen bonds formed between the PMMA and CDs promote the generation of RTP. The photo-responsiveness of RTP is caused by the conversion of molecular oxygen (3O2) to singlet oxygen (1O2) under continuous UV irradiation. It is filled with 3O2 again after being placed in air to inactivate the RTP, thus achieving reversible RTP behavior. Owing to their excellent photo-responsive properties and water resistance, these materials have the potential to achieve repeatable dynamic information encryption and multilevel pattern anti-counterfeiting while also broadening the range of applications in water environments.

head.ce:title: Photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive room temperature phosphorescence carbon dot-based composites with excellent reversibility and water resistance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Rizhao, Shandong 276826 PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Rizhao, Shandong 276826 PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-stimulation responsive room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have potential applications in advanced dynamic information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. However, the development of photo-responsive long-wavelength RTP CD materials with excellent reversibility and water resistance, as well as CD materials with both photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP properties, remains a challenge. Here, pyrene, which has a large conjugated structure, and 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde, which has a tetraphenylethylene structure, were selected as the main raw materials to synthesize two CDs. Then, by introducing a PMMA matrix, reversible photo-responsive long-wavelength red RTP emission with a lifetime of 257 ms from a CD-based composite is realized for the first time, and the combination of photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible yellow RTP with a lifetime of 287 ms is realized. Importantly, photo-responsive reversible RTP has excellent water resistance. The fluorescence discoloration was caused by photocyclization under long-term UV irradiation due to the presence of the tetraphenylethylene structure. The rigid environment provided by the PMMA matrix and the hydrogen bonds formed between the PMMA and CDs promote the generation of RTP. The photo-responsiveness of RTP is caused by the conversion of molecular oxygen (3O2) to singlet oxygen (1O2) under continuous UV irradiation. It is filled with 3O2 again after being placed in air to inactivate the RTP, thus achieving reversible RTP behavior. Owing to their excellent photo-responsive properties and water resistance, these materials have the potential to achieve repeatable dynamic information encryption and multilevel pattern anti-counterfeiting while also broadening the range of applications in water environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting and information encryption have always been important measures for protecting society and people's lives. With the progress and development of society, it is no longer limited to a single anti-counterfeiting and information encryption mode, and advanced dynamic information encryption and multilevel anti-counterfeiting technology have gradually proliferated. Therefore, the exploration of anti-counterfeiting functional materials with dynamic response characteristics is increasingly important. Unlike traditional fluorescent materials that can respond only immediately, phosphorescent materials with dynamic stimulus responses show switchable optical properties and have great application potential in the field of multilevel anti-counterfeiting and advanced dynamic information encryption [1]. In recent years, dynamic polymer-based RTP materials with stimulus responses have been the focus of research. External stimuli such as excitation wavelength, temperature, humidity and ultraviolet (UV) irradiation can all produce stimulus responses to specific polymer-based RTP materials [2]. At present, important achievements have been made in research on the stimulus-responsive characteristics of organic RTP materials, but the reasonable design and manufacture of organic RTP materials still face challenges [3–6]. In addition, most polymer-based RTP materials usually undergo RTP quenching in aqueous environments, which limits their application in aqueous environments [7,8]. Therefore, the design and manufacture of stimulus-responsive RTP materials with simple processes and excellent water resistance are urgently needed.

Carbon dots (CDs) have excellent fluorescence and phosphorescence properties, and their preparation process is simple and convenient [9–11]. In recent years, dynamic RTP CD materials have become a new star in the field of stimulus-responsive materials, mainly through the introduction of a polymer matrix to achieve dynamic RTP properties of CD-based composites. Among various external stimuli, UV irradiation is an effective way to achieve the dynamic photo-responsive characteristics of CD-based composites, which exhibit a switchable on–off optical response [12]. The dynamic ultra-long RTP flexible solid film prepared by Jia's team from CDs and polyvinylpyrrolidone achieved UV activation and thermal inactivation, which was successfully applied to time–temperature indication logistics labels and enriched the dynamic RTP material library [13]. Zhu's team constructed a green RTP system activated by multiple stimuli of light, acid and heat on a single CD system. Their photo-activated RTP behavior was caused by the photoexcitation-induced aggregation of CDs [14]. Sun's team reported the photo-induced RTP of CDs in porous gelatin films, in which green RTP was activated after a few seconds of light exposure and recovered after sitting in air for some time, with potential applications in optical patterns and data security [15]. Despite these significant achievements, developing long-wavelength RTP CD materials with reversible photo-responsive properties, as well as CD-based materials simultaneously exhibiting photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and reversible RTP characteristics, remains a daunting challenge.

On the basis of the above issues, we selected suitable precursors and a matrix material to achieve, for the first time, photo-responsive reversible long-wavelength red RTP emission of CD-based composite materials. We also realized the combination of photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP properties. The relevant RTP characteristics exhibit excellent water resistance. In this study, two kinds of CDs (Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs) were first prepared, and then two kinds of photo-responsive CD-based composites (Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA) were prepared by introducing a PMMA matrix. Py-CDs@PMMA displays a photo-responsive reversible red RTP, whereas TPVBA-CDs@PMMA achieves both photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible yellow RTP. Here, the PMMA matrix was chosen because of its strong rigidity, excellent oxygen permeability, and waterproofing properties, making it an excellent matrix material for promoting the dynamic photo-responsive RTP of CDs and achieving RTP in aqueous solutions. Through a series of literature studies and material characterization, it has been proven that the photo-responsive RTP characteristics are caused by the conversion of 3O2 to 1O2 under continuous UV irradiation, and RTP is deactivated after a period of time in air to achieve reversible RTP behavior. The existence of a large conjugated structure and the hydrogen bonds formed between the PMMA matrix and Py-CDs enable Py-CDs@PMMA to achieve long-wavelength RTP emission. The fluorescence discoloration of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA may be caused by photocyclization under long-term UV irradiation. The RTP emission is not quenched in aqueous solution, resulting in excellent water resistance, which is closely related to the waterproofing of the PMMA matrix. On the basis of photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP behavior, repeatable dynamic information encryption and a multilevel pattern anti-counterfeiting mode are designed, which shows potential for application in the field of advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption and expands the application range in water environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP CD-based composites
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, RTP composites with photo-responsive properties were obtained by embedding CDs in a polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) matrix. First, two kinds of CDs were prepared via a microwave solid-phase reaction method. The blue fluorescent CDs synthesized with pyrene, hexadecanedioic acid, and hydroxyurea as precursors and AlCl3·6H2O as the catalyst and heated in a microwave oven at medium–low heat for 20 min are referred to as Py-CDs. The green fluorescent CDs synthesized with 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde and hexadecanedioic acid as precursors and AlCl3·6H2O as the catalyst heated for 10 min at medium–low heat and then heated for 15 min at medium heat are referred to as TPVBA-CDs. Then, the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were mixed with a PMMA matrix solution to prepare Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA composite films, respectively. Py-CDs@PMMA emits blue fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, and there is no RTP signal after the UV light is turned on and immediately turned off. Unexpectedly, after continuous UV irradiation for 30 s followed by turning off, Py-CDs@PMMA exhibited red RTP. After approximately 10 min of exposure to air, it reverted to a state without RTP, demonstrating excellent photo-responsive reversible red RTP characteristics. In addition, we were surprised to find that the initial state of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA (referred to as TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0) exhibited cyan fluorescence under UV irradiation. However, after 20 min of continuous UV irradiation (referred to as TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20), the fluorescence color changed to blue. TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 has no RTP emission, whereas TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 produced an enhanced yellow RTP after 20 min of irradiation. Like Py-CDs@PMMA, TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 also exhibited photo-responsive reversible yellow RTP properties. The RTP of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 is very weak and almost invisible after the UV light is turned on and immediately turned off, but the yellow RTP is significantly enhanced after 10 s of continuous UV irradiation, and the yellow RTP disappears after being placed in air for approximately 1 h. Importantly, the RTP of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 was not quenched after soaking in water for 12 h, indicating excellent water resistance (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Both Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were prepared via a microwave solid-phase reaction method. Pyrene and hydroxyurea are selected as raw materials for the synthesis of Py-CDs because pyrene contains large conjugated aromatic rings, which are conducive to realizing long-wavelength emission [16]. Hydroxyurea contains N and O heteroatoms, and the doping of N and O atoms is conducive to improving the properties of CDs [17]. The presence of hydroxyl and amino groups is conducive to the existence of hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the formed CDs, which easily forms hydrogen bonds with the PMMA matrix. The choice of 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde as the main raw material for the synthesis of TPVBA-CDs was made because 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde contains a tetraphenylethylene structure, the phenomenon of fluorescence discoloration may occur [18], and the presence of CO is conducive to the enhancement of spin-orbital coupling and provides the basis for the generation of RTP [19]. In addition, studies have shown that the presence of alkyl chains can prevent the direct contact of luminous centers to achieve solid-state luminescence [20], so hexadecanedioic acid was added to the synthesis of Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs to ensure the solid-state luminescence of the CDs. We studied the morphology and chemical structure of the CDs through a series of characterization techniques. The morphologies of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1 a and b). Both the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs are nearly spherical nanoparticles with average sizes of 2.73 nm and 2.43 nm, respectively. As shown in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, the Py-CDs have a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [21]. The lattice spacing of the TPVBA-CDs is 0.2 nm, which also corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [22].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) images (Fig. 1c) show that Py-CDs have two sharp peaks at 2θ = 21.33° and 2θ = 23.15°, both of which correspond to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. There is a smaller and weaker peak at 2θ = 40.43°, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. Similarly, the TPVBA-CDs show two strong sharp peaks corresponding to the graphitic carbon (002) crystal plane at 2θ = 21.37° and 2θ = 23.74°. The weak peak at 2θ = 40.51° also corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [23–25]. The peaks corresponding to the graphitic carbon (100) crystal plane at 2θ = 40.43° and 2θ = 40.51° for the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs match the lattice spacing of the HRTEM images of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs. The XRD patterns show that both the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs exhibit sharp diffraction peaks, indicating their good crystallinity.

The defects and graphitization degrees of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were also characterized via Raman spectroscopy. Fig. S1 shows the Raman spectra of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs. The peaks of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs at 1404 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1 and at 1424 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 are D and G bands, respectively [26,27]. The D band is associated with disordered sp3 carbon, and the G band is caused by the stretching of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The D-band to G-band strength ratio of the Py-CDs was 1.47, indicating that there was a certain degree of structural defects. The D band of the TPVBA-CDs is weak, with an intensity ratio of the D to G band of 0.29, which further reveals that the degree of graphitization is relatively high. In addition, the Py-CDs also show two distinct peaks at 1239 and 1627 cm−1, which may be caused by the presence of –COOH and CC bonds [28,29]. This also indicates that the Py-CDs have graphitized carbon nuclei, which may contain carboxyl functional groups on their surface. The TPVBA-CDs also show a weak peak at 1476 cm−1, which may be caused by the vibration coupling deviation of the carbon bonds near the sp3 defect site [30].

Next, the functional group structures of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were identified via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 1d, the Py-CDs mainly exhibited two distinct absorption bands at 1640 cm−1 and 3440 cm−1. The absorption at 1640 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of CC/CO, and the absorption at 3440 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of the NH/OH bond [31,32]. The results revealed that there may be carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino functional groups on the surface of the Py-CDs. The TPVBA-CDs also have two distinct absorption bands at 1640 cm−1 and 3420 cm−1. The absorption at 1640 cm−1 is also due to the stretching vibration of CC/CO, and the absorption band at 3420 cm−1 is due mainly to OH bonds. There may be carboxyl or aldehyde functional groups on the surface of the TPVBA-CDs. In addition, the FTIR data of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs did not revral the functional groups or chemical bonds associated with AlCl3·6H2O, so the synthesized CDs did not contain the added catalyst AlCl3·6H2O.

The elemental compositions and chemical states of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were further characterized via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full XPS spectra of the Py-CDs show peaks corresponding to C (72.91 %), N (25.94 %) and O (1.15 %) at 285.0 eV, 400.0 eV and 533.0 eV, respectively (Fig. S2a, Table S1). The high-resolution C 1s spectra of the Py-CDs were deconvoluted into peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to CC/ CC, CO, and CO, respectively (Fig. S2b) [33]. The high-resolution O 1s spectra were deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.3 eV and 534.0 eV, corresponding to CO and COH, respectively (Fig. S2c) [34,35]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra have two peaks at 399.7 eV (CN) and 400.9 eV (NH) (Fig. S2d) [35]. The TPVBA-CDs were composed of C (76.86 %) and O (23.14 %) (Fig. S2a, Table S1). There are three peaks in the high-resolution C 1s spectra, corresponding to CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.6 eV) and CO (288.9 eV) (Fig. S2e). The high-resolution O 1s spectra have two peaks at 532.3 eV (CO) and 534.0 eV (COH) (Fig. S2f). The XPS spectra further prove that there are abundant functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which provides favorable conditions for the subsequent realization of the RTP properties of the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were studied via ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, Py-CDs have two obvious UV absorption peaks at 271 nm and 327 nm. The absorption peak at 271 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 carbon conjugation domain, which indicates that Py-CDs may have a large conjugated domain carbon core structure [36]. The absorption peak at 327 nm is related to the n-π* transition of CN/CO bonds, indicating that the surface of the Py-CDs contains amino and carboxyl functional groups [37]. The large conjugation domain carbon core and surface functional groups may lay a foundation for the realization of long-wavelength RTP of the Py-CDs@PMMA composite film. The absorption peaks of the TPVBA-CDs at 264 nm and 291 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition of CC in the aromatic chromophor structure, and the absorption peak at 328 nm is caused by the n-π* transition of CO, which may come from surface carboxyl or aldehyde groups [36,38,39].

To further understand the fluorescence properties of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs, the liquid and solid fluorescence spectra were studied. The Py-CDs solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 28.31 % under 365 nm UV irradiation, and the emission wavelength was 419 nm (Fig. S3a and e). The emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths revealed that the Py-CDs solution exhibited excitation-independent properties, the fluorescence intensity reached a maximum at the optimal excitation wavelength of 378 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was 419 nm (Fig. S3b and c). The excitation-independent photoluminescence of CDs is due mainly to surface state/defect emission [40]. The Py-CDs powder exhibited blue fluorescence with a QY of 3.67 % at a wavelength of 466 nm under irradiation with 365 nm UV light and showed excitation-independent properties (Fig. 2dii, Fig. S3d and f), and the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were 376 nm and 468 nm, respectively (Fig. 2b). Compared with that of liquid fluorescence, the emission wavelength of the solid-state fluorescence of Py-CDs is redshifted, possibly because the π-π stacking interactions in solid-state CDs increase energy transfer and generate new electron-state emission in the long-wavelength region [41]. The TPVBA-CDs powder exhibited green fluorescence at a wavelength of 506 nm under irradiation with 365 nm UV light and showed excitation-independent properties (Fig. 2div, Fig. S4a and b), and the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were 407 nm and 509 nm, respectively (Fig. 2c). Surprisingly, the TPVBA-CDs solution showed extremely weak blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, which was barely observable (Fig. S4c and f). We further analyzed its weak fluorescence and found that it is also excitation-independent (Fig. S4d). The fluorescence intensity was the highest at 391 nm excitation, and the emission wavelength was 394 nm (Fig. S4e). The TPVBA-CDs emit strong fluorescence with a QY of 16.27 % in the solid-state but weak fluorescence with a QY of only 0.21 % in solution, which may be due to the aggregation-induced emission property of the tetraphenylethylene molecular structure in the precursor material 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde, which endows the prepared TPVBA-CDs with aggregation-induced emission properties. Compared with dispersions in solvents, TPVBA-CDs have limited intramolecular motion in the solid aggregate state, resulting in strong fluorescence emission [42,43]. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs were calculated according to Equation (S1) to be 14.94 ns and 2.71 ns, respectively (Fig. 2e). The commission internationale eclairage (CIE) coordinates corresponding to the solid-state fluorescence of the Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs are (0.18, 0.22) and (0.29, 0.47), respectively, which are consistent with the blue fluorescence and green fluorescence (Fig. 2f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the photophysical properties of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA composites
ce:section.ce:para: Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs contain abundant surface functional groups, which provide the basis for achieving RTP emission by introducing a matrix. Therefore, we selected PMMA with good oxygen permeability and waterproofing as the matrix and combined it with Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs to prepare Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA composite films. The specific synthesis process is described in detail in the supporting information. To determine the amount of the PMMA matrix, we studied the effects of different proportions of the PMMA matrix on Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA. The Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA composite films were prepared with 100 mg/ml, 200 mg/ml and 300 mg/ml PMMA matrixes, respectively. When the PMMA matrix concentration was 200 mg/ml, Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA exhibited excellent photo-responsive reversible RTP properties. However, larger or smaller concentrations do not result in photo-responsive RTP characteristics, and the fluorescence properties are relatively inferior to those of 200 mg/ml (Fig. S5). Therefore, we chose to prepare Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA using a PMMA matrix with a concentration of 200 mg/ml for subsequent investigations.

To demonstrate the successful binding of the CDs to the PMMA matrix, the XRD and XPS spectra of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA were obtained. The XRD patterns show that Py-CDs@PMMA has two wide peaks at 2θ = 15.87° and 2θ = 29.27° (Fig. 3 a, black line). TPVBA-CDs@PMMA also has two broad peaks at 2θ = 16.37° and 2θ = 29.78° (Fig. 3b, cyan line). Compared with the sharp peaks in the XRD patterns of the CDs, there are significant differences in the composite films, indicating that the CDs are successfully embedded in the PMMA matrix. In addition, the XPS spectra of the composite films are significantly different from those of the CDs, which further indicates that the CDs and the PMMA matrix are successfully combined. As shown in Fig. S6a, Py-CDs@PMMA contains C (66.48 %), O (32.54 %), and N (0.97 %) (Table S1). The high-resolution C 1s spectra contain three peaks: 284.8 eV (CC/ CC), 286.8 eV (CO), and 288.6 eV (CO) (Fig. S6b). The high-resolution O 1s spectra contain two peaks at 532.1 eV (CO) and 533.6 eV (CO) (Fig. S6c). The high-resolution N 1s spectra contain two peaks: 399.3 eV (CN) and 400.6 eV (NH) (Fig. S6d). TPVBA-CDs@PMMA contains C (61.91 %) and O (38.09 %) (Fig. S6e, Table S1). The high-resolution C 1s spectra contain three peaks: CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.4 eV), and CO (288.6 eV) (Fig. S6f). The high-resolution O 1s spectra contain two peaks: CO (532.0 eV) and CO (533.5 eV) (Fig. S6g).

Then, the photophysical properties of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA were further investigated via fluorescence and phosphorescence spectroscopy. We found that Py-CDs@PMMA exhibited blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, with two emission peaks at 393 nm and 413 nm, with a QY of 8.49 % (Fig. S7a). And the fluorescence exhibited excitation-independent emission (Fig. S7b). After UV irradiation was immediately turned off (UV on for 0 s), no RTP emission from Py-CDs@PMMA occurred. After continuous irradiation for 30 s (UV on for 30 s), red RTP emission was visible, with two major peaks of RTP emission at 592 nm and 654 nm (Fig. 4 a and j). After being placed in air for approximately 10 min, the red RTP disappeared and reappeared after 30 s of continuous irradiation, indicating reversible photo-responsive RTP (Fig. 4e). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that a CD material has achieved photo-responsive reversible long-wavelength red RTP emission (Table S2). Surprisingly, the fluorescence wavelength of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA blueshifted and the fluorescence intensity decreased after continuous UV irradiation (Fig. 4b). TPVBA-CDs@PMMA shows cyan fluorescence with a QY of 17.69 % under UV irradiation in the initial state (TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0), and the emission wavelength is 502 nm (Fig. S7c). However, after continuous UV irradiation for 20 min (TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20), the signal became blue fluorescence with a QY of 5.41 % at a wavelength of 487 nm (Fig. S7d). The fluorescence emission of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 exhibited excitation-independent properties (Fig. S7e and f). Moreover, TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 has almost no RTP, whereas TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 shows a significantly enhanced yellow RTP (Fig. 4c and k). The RTP of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 is very weak and almost invisible after the UV light is immediately turned on and off. After 10 s of continuous UV irradiation, the yellow RTP emission at a wavelength of 554 nm is significantly enhanced (Fig. 4d and k). After being placed in air for approximately 1 h, the yellow RTP disappeared and reappeared after being irradiated again, also indicating good reversibility (Fig. 4f). We also achieved the combination of fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP in CD materials, which has not been reported in previous studies. Importantly, the RTPs of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 have good water resistance, and photo-responsive reversible RTP still occurs in aqueous solutions (Fig. S7g and h). The CIE coordinates of the RTP emissions of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 are (0.60, 0.37) and (0.42, 0.51), respectively, corresponding well to the red and yellow regions (Fig. 4g). To further demonstrate that RTP is generated by the embedding of Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs, which are formed by the carbonization of pyrene and 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde under a microwave solid-phase reaction into the PMMA matrix, we investigated the optical properties of the pyrene/hexadecanedioic acid/hydroxyurea/PMMA mixture (Py@PMMA) and the 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde/hexadecanedioic acid/PMMA mixture (TPVBA@PMMA). As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence of Py@PMMA and TPVBA@PMMA is similar to that of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA, and TPVBA@PMMA also exhibits photo-induced fluorescence discoloration. However, Py@PMMA and TPVBA@PMMA do not produce RTP. This further indicates that the precursor materials underwent carbonization under microwave solid-phase reactions to form Py-CDs and TPVBA-CDs, and that the embedding of CDs in the PMMA matrix enables Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA to exhibit photo-responsive RTP phenomena. In addition, we calculated through Equation (S1) that the fluorescence lifetimes of Py-CDs@PMMA, TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 were 9.32 ns, 3.97 ns and 5.63 ns, respectively (Fig. S7i). According to Equation (S2), the RTP lifetimes of Py-CDs@PMMA after 30 s of UV irradiation and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 after 10 s of UV irradiation are 257 ms and 287 ms, respectively (Fig. 4h and i). The RTP lifetime of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 after 10 s of UV irradiation was significantly greater than that before irradiation (0.43 ms) (Fig. 4i). In addition, the RTP QYs of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 after UV irradiation are 10.57 % and 4.43 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we investigated the mechanism by which Py-CDs@PMMA produces photo-responsive reversible red RTP and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA produces fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP. First, the long-wavelength red RTP produced by Py-CDs@PMMA is closely related to the large conjugated domain structure formed by the Py-CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra show that Py-CDs have a large conjugated domain structure (Fig. 2a), which provides a basis for realizing long-wavelength RTP. In addition, the position of the hydroxyl group (3480 cm−1) in the FTIR spectrum of Py-CDs@PMMA is offset compared with that of Py-CDs (3440 cm−1), indicating that hydrogen bonds are formed between the Py-CDs and the PMMA matrix (Fig. S9a black line, Fig. 1d) [44]. Therefore, Py-CDs@PMMA achieves long-wavelength red RTP. On the one hand, pyrene was selected as the raw material to form a large conjugated structure of Py-CDs. On the other hand, the amino, hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of the Py-CDs can form hydrogen bonds with the PMMA matrix. Additionally, PMMA provides a strong rigid environment that facilitates intersystem crossing (ISC) processes from the single state (S1) to the triplet state (T1) and inhibits non-radiative transitions of triplet excitons, thereby enabling long-wavelength RTP emission (Fig. 5 a). To further investigate the cause of the photo-responsive reversible RTP of Py-CDs@PMMA, FTIR spectra and XRD patterns were obtained after 0 s and 30 s of UV irradiation. The FTIR spectra show little difference before and after irradiation, and the structure was essentially the same, indicating that no new chemical bonds were formed (Fig. S9a). Similarly, the XRD patterns did not change before and after irradiation, which further indicates that the structure of Py-CDs@PMMA does not change before and after irradiation (Fig. 3a). Therefore, on the basis of a series of characterizations and literature investigations, we conclude that the photo-responsive RTP of Py-CDs@PMMA occurs because 3O2 diffuses in the Py-CDs@PMMA film in the initial state, and most triplet excitons interact with 3O2 and are quenched, thereby preventing the observation of RTP. After continuous UV irradiation, 3O2 is consumed and converted into 1O2, thus activating RTP [1,45,46]. The excellent oxygen permeability of the PMMA matrix is the key to achieving photo-responsive RTP. After a period of time in the air, the RTP disappears because it is filled with 3O2, thus making it reversible (Fig. 5a). This conclusion is also supported by the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of Py-CDs@PMMA before and after UV irradiation (Fig. S10a). After continuous exposure to UV irradiation in air, the 1O2 signal of Py-CDs@PMMA is enhanced, indicating the conversion of 3O2 to 1O2 [1,47].

To determine the reasons for the fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive RTP of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA, we characterized the TPVBA-CDs@PMMA before and after irradiation. As shown in Fig. S11, the UV–vis absorption spectra of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA exhibited significant changes before and after 20 min of UV irradiation. After irradiation, the absorption peak at 223 nm and 279 nm weakened, the peak at 294 nm increased, and a new absorption band appeared at 362 nm, indicating that photochemical reactions may have occurred under long-term UV irradiation, resulting in structural changes [48]. To further demonstrate this, we analyzed FTIR, XRD and XPS before and after irradiation of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA. A comparison of the FTIR spectra before 20 min of irradiation (Fig. S9b, black line) with those after 20 min of irradiation (Fig. S9c, black line) revealed that the C-H stretching of the saturated hydrocarbon group at 2970 cm−1 was more obvious after 20 min of irradiation. This may be due to the existence of a tetraphenylethylene structure on the surface of the TPVBA-CDs caused by the use of 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde as the raw material, which underwent photocyclization after continuous irradiation for 20 min, resulting in the conversion of unsaturated hydrocarbon C-H into saturated hydrocarbon C-H [18]. A comparison of the XRD patterns before irradiation (Fig. 3b, cyan line) and after irradiation (Fig. 3c, blue line) revealed that the XRD peak at 2θ = 16.37° decreased after continuous irradiation for 20 min. In addition, we characterized the XPS spectrum of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20. TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 also contains C (69.46 %) and O (30.54 %) (Fig. 3d, Table S1). The high-resolution C 1s spectra contain three peaks: CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.5 eV), and CO (288.5 eV) (Fig. 3e). The high-resolution O 1s spectra contain two peaks: CO (531.5 eV) and CO (532.7 eV) (Fig. 3f). After 20 min of irradiation, the content of CC/CC in TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 increased compared with that in TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 (Table S3), possibly because CC decreased and CC increased after photocyclization, and the increase in CC was greater than the decrease in CC. Therefore, the overall trend is increasing. The changes in the XRD and XPS results before and after irradiation further indicate that long-term UV irradiation may lead to photocyclization of the tetraphenylethylene structure on the surface of the TPVBA-CDs, resulting in changes in fluorescence color (Fig. 5b).

TPVBA-CDs@PMMA has no RTP before UV irradiation and a significantly enhanced yellow RTP after fluorescence discoloration. Compared with the FTIR spectra, the hydroxyl group position of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 (3420 cm−1) shows no change compared with that of TPVBA-CDs (3420 cm−1) (Fig. S9b black line, Fig. 1d), indicating that no hydrogen bonds formed between PMMA and the TPVBA-CDs at this time or that the hydrogen bond strength was extremely weak. After 10 s of irradiation, there was essentially no difference in the position of the FTIR spectral absorption peak (Fig. S9b red line) and no change in the XRD pattern (Fig. 3b red line). A short irradiation time is not enough to cause photocyclization, so the fluorescence was still cyan at this time, and there was essentially no RTP. After long-term irradiation, on the one hand, photo-driven molecular motion can reduce the distance between adjacent benzene rings and increase the interaction between molecules, thus promoting the ISC process and stabilizing the excited triplet state, thus producing a visible RTP effect [49]. On the other hand, the position of the hydroxyl group in the FTIR spectrum of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 (3480 cm−1) is shifted compared with that of the TPVBA-CDs (3420 cm−1) (Fig. S9c black line, Fig. 1d). These findings indicate that the carboxyl groups on the surface of the TPVBA-CDs may form strong hydrogen bonds with the PMMA matrix and that the rigid environment formed by PMMA inhibits the non-radiative inactivation of triplet excitons, resulting in a significant increase in RTP (Fig. 5a). For TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 with reversible photo-responsive RTP, we also characterized its FTIR spectra and XRD patterns after irradiation for 0 s and 10 s. Similar to Py-CDs@PMMA, there was basically no difference in the FTIR spectra and XRD patterns after 0 s and 10 s of irradiation (Fig. S9c, Fig. 3c). Therefore, the mechanism behind the photo-responsive reversible yellow RTP generated by TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 is the same as that generated by Py-CDs@PMMA, which is also caused by the conversion of 3O2 into 1O2 under continuous UV irradiation (Fig. 5a). The EPR spectra of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 before and after UV irradiation further support this conclusion (Fig. S10b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatable dynamic information encryption and multilevel anti-counterfeiting application
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP properties of the Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA films, anti-counterfeiting and information encryption modes were designed, and their potential applications in advanced dynamic information encryption and pattern anti-counterfeiting were demonstrated. As shown in Fig. 6 , reversible dynamic information encryption applications are designed using three films: Py-CDs@PMMA, TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20. Py-CDs@PMMA, TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0 and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 show “2889″ with different fluorescence colors under UV irradiation (Fig. 6ai, Fig. 6bi), and the information displayed by ”2889″ in this case is false. After the UV light was turned on and immediately turned off (UV radiation for 0 s), the “2889″ signal disappeared (Fig. 6aii, Fig. 6bii). Then, the UV radiation is turned off after continuous irradiation for 10 s, and a yellow RTP appears at TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20. The ”11″ message that appears at this point is also false (Fig. 6aiii, Fig. 6biii). However, when UV irradiation is turned off after 30 s, the red RTP and yellow RTP of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20 appear at the same time, and true information “2083″ is obtained (Fig. 6aiv, Fig. 6biv). Since red RTP and yellow RTP are reversible and can be recovered after being placed in air, this dynamic information encryption process can be repeated in cycles (Fig. 6b). As shown in Fig. S12, we designed a photo-responsive dynamic multilevel pattern anti-counterfeiting application based on different UV irradiation times. We used three kinds of composite materials and molds to design a pattern containing a heart shape (TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-0), two small dolphin shapes (TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20) and a circular shape (Py-CDs@PMMA). Under irradiation with 365 nm UV, the three shapes all showed fluorescence, and pattern 1 was the first-level anti-counterfeiting pattern. The pattern disappears after the UV light is immediately turned off (second-level anti-counterfeiting). The UV light is continuously irradiated for 10 s, only two yellow dolphin shapes appear, and pattern 2 is a third-level anti-counterfeiting pattern. After 30 s of continuous UV irradiation, four-level anti-counterfeiting pattern 3, that is, two yellow dolphins and a red circle, is observed. Thus, dynamic multilevel anti-counterfeiting of the pattern can be realized. In addition, owing to the excellent water resistance of the RTP of Py-CDs@PMMA and TPVBA-CDs@PMMA-20, we can still realize the application of the pattern after soaking with water, which shows great potential for application in water environments.

In conclusion, by introducing a PMMA matrix, we achieved photo-responsive reversible long-wavelength red RTP emission of CD-based composites for the first time and realized the combination of photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP properties. Py-CDs@PMMA displays a photo-responsive reversible red RTP, whereas TPVBA-CDs@PMMA achieves both photo-induced fluorescence discoloration and a photo-responsive reversible yellow RTP. Importantly, RTP can still hold up well in aqueous solutions. The presence of large conjugated structures and the formation of hydrogen bond interactions result in the long-wavelength red RTP of Py-CDs@PMMA. The photo-responsive reversible RTP phenomenon occurs because PMMA provides a rigid environment for CDs, and hydrogen bond interactions are formed between CDs and the PMMA matrix, which inhibits the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons. The PMMA matrix has good oxygen permeability. In the initial state, 3O2 fills the entire film, resulting in no RTP. After continuous UV irradiation, 3O2 is converted into 1O2, resulting in the activation of RTP. The fluorescence discoloration of TPVBA-CDs@PMMA is due to the presence of tetraphenylethylene, which results in photocyclization under long-term UV irradiation. On the basis of the phenomenon of fluorescence discoloration and photo-responsive reversible RTP with excellent water resistance, we designed repeatable dynamic information encryption and multilevel pattern anti-counterfeiting modes, which show potential for application in the field of advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption and also broaden the application range in water environments.

Shouhong Sun: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Qiang Fu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhanhua Dong: Formal analysis. Mingbo Yue: Formal analysis.
